message:
fix(resources): scan directory contents for reservation activity detection

The reservation system tracks file activity to determine whether a
reserved resource is still being actively worked on. Previously,
when a reservation pattern matched a directory, only the directory's
own mtime was checked. On most filesystems, a directory's mtime only
updates when entries are added or removed directly inside it—not when
files within subdirectories are modified. This meant that an agent
actively editing files deep inside a reserved directory tree would
appear idle, causing its reservation to be considered stale.

The fix introduces recursive content scanning for directory
reservations: when the matched candidate is a directory, we construct
a glob pattern (pattern/**) and walk the directory tree to find the
most recently modified file. The directory's own mtime is used as a
fallback only if the recursive scan finds nothing.

Additionally, reservation_glob_walk_latest_micros() now accepts a
separate start_dir parameter instead of always starting from the
workspace root. This is necessary because directory reservation scans
need to start from the matched directory itself (not the workspace
root), while the existing glob-pattern fallback path still needs to
start from the workspace root. The caller at the original call site
passes workspace for both parameters to preserve existing behavior.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
